name: Cova des Genet
starting_point: Due to recent access problems to Cala Varques, check conditions before visiting. Alternatively, approach from the coast via Cala Romántica.
equipment: Vertical progression equipment and 12-13 m of rope required.
description: Discovered in 2008 near Cala Varques, this cave has a small 50x70 cm entrance leading to a 6-meter descent into a spacious main chamber approximately 27 meters wide. The hall is richly decorated with columns surrounded by dry gours, and a ceiling covered with thousands of fine stalactites. Uniquely, the cave is formed in sandstone rather than limestone, distinguishing it from neighboring caves in the area.
full_description:
While cycling in August with his mountain bike through the Cala Varques area, F. Bonnin, a dear companion of many "adventures" both in the island's mountains and in the Pyrenean mountain range, by chance found the entrance to the cave, so he did not hesitate to record the location coordinates with his GPS as he usually does each time he finds something, and immediately upon arriving home he called me by phone to inform me of the discovery in case it was of interest to me. The most curious thing for me was discovering that nothing cataloged was to be found at that location despite the area being sufficiently frequented by both tourists, speleologists, and hikers. On August 10th, after a visit to the Cala Falcó cave, I passed by the location to identify the cave entrance and get an idea of what I was going to find there, which I certainly did not guess, since the idea that slipped into my head in no way corresponded with the reality I found on my first visit.

For a long time, the cave also known as Cova des Xots, present in the area, has been known — apparently incorrectly — as Cova des Genet. A long time ago, J. Aguiló began cataloging the caves of the island of Mallorca, noting in a book by C. Veny their location and referring to a cave in this area of cathedral-like dimensions with the name Genet. It is clear that J. Aguiló exaggerated a bit in this description since, although the cave is of magnitude III, the dimensions, particularly of its main hall, do not quite reach that level. The fact is that, unless someone proves otherwise, the Cova des Genet is not the one that was previously believed to be so, but rather the one we are discussing here.

First Exploration

On September 7th, 2008, accompanied by A. Villar, J. A. Encinas, and M. L. Redondo, we set out to explore and also to survey the cave, but we only accomplished the first of these, in addition to photographing the cave, leaving the topography pending for a later visit, as we did not expect to find a cave of those dimensions and characteristics.

Second Exploration

On October 5th, 2008, a group consisting of J. A. Encinas, G. Herraez, J. A. Pérez, and myself accessed the cave with the intention of surveying it, always under the direction of J. A. Encinas.

Description

The entrance to the cave is located next to one of the trails that, starting from the beach of Cala Varques, border the coast in the direction of Cala Falcó. The dimensions of the cave entrance are 50 x 70 cm, and as we make our way into the interior of the 6-meter-deep shaft, it gradually widens, finally ending the descent with an overhang that deposits us on a pile of debris in which, alongside the stones that have been thrown in from the outside, there are bottles, cans, and bones of pigs and other animals whose carcasses were thrown by humans into the cave, since it is difficult, though not impossible, that some fell inside due to the small dimensions of the entrance.

Once "landed," we find ourselves in a small vestibule of almost circular shape where the first concretions are already visible, notably a small column a little more than 1.5 m from the floor near the place where the cave continues through a descending gallery with a slippery floor depending on the time of year. The cave "drops" from right to left, and on the right side a great quantity of formations and numerous stones from some collapses can be appreciated. Fractured column remnants can also be seen, as in some other parts of the cave, possibly from some terrain adjustment. The slope is quite steep while the cave simultaneously narrows. On the opposite side, however, access is much easier despite the numerous stones that carpet the floor, among which some vegetal "stalagmites" can be observed, which are also abundant in the Cova de s'Embut.

As we continue advancing, a small room appears to our left, a little before arriving at the great hall of this cave, where we will have no choice but to marvel at such beauty. Approximately 27 meters wide, this hall is filled with columns, some of them surrounded by gours unfortunately empty during both my visits, with a ceiling from which thousands of the finest stalactites hang, some of which can reach one meter in length, interspersed with some of greater thickness. On the north side of the hall, a small pool (dry at the time of the visit) serves as the cave's sinkhole where the water from a well-marked stream is discharged, collecting it from different points. The mark on the wall showing the level the water reached at some point is visible. A small room is found at the end of the main hall with no continuity, and another one hides behind a slope further to the right, accessed without any difficulty. Many of the columns in this cave are joined in two pieces, possibly from a terrain subsidence, but subsequently the column was completed again.

We have tried to find the connection between this cave and its neighbor the Cova des Colom II but it has not been possible to find it, although that does not mean it does not exist.

In addition to the previously mentioned curious vegetal "stalagmites," different speleothems hide in the corners of the cave, where it is even possible to observe some scarce eccentric stalactites.

From the file on the Cova des Xenet, or des Genet, J. A. Encinas (CCMallorca, unpublished work, 1,400 pages) provides me with the following extract:

"... C. Veny reproduces, in his 1968 work, the list of caves from the J. Aguiló archive, including the Cova des Genet, of which he says it has a 'small opening and inside, like a church.' If, as we believe, this is the present cave in the surroundings of Son Forteza de Manacor, the cited scholar must have interpreted the phoneme of the toponym in relation to the site being situated right on a hillock, at the orographic saddle between Cala Varques and Cala Falcó. But xenet was formerly a significant nautical term, a type of thick rope already quite frayed on the surface from use; xenilla in Catalan, when it is woven on purpose to leave fringes and make the rope comfortable to handle for a good grip. A type of rope still common in the verticalized accesses of some shafts and in certain caves. So in the referenced Cova des Genet, the scant ten meters of vertical drop could in its time be overcome by going down by arms through the opening, with feet against the wall, until the overhang of the bottom section required grasping the very specific quality of the fuzzy rope. Once in the spacious interior hall, one verifies the descriptive value of the simile used by the cleric J. Aguiló, as beautiful and powerful columns abound, along with dense zenith concentrations of fistular stalactites, eccentric filigrees, some singular vegetal formations formed under the shelter of punctual water drippings, and barely any archaeological evidence of anthropic frequentation, save for some fragments of nearly contemporary ceramics. The cavern is aligned with the immediate marine cave of Cala Varques (Coloms, C.10), from which it is separated underground by some fifty meters, following the same structural discontinuity and developing a gallery of subsidential character with appreciable vertical glyptochemical progression, reaching in Xenet (X.06) 74 m of projected length, with a maximum width of 32 and a total vertical drop on the order of a dozen..."

Unlike its neighbors in the area, the rock from which the Cova des Genet is formed is not limestone, but sandstone — a detrital sedimentary rock of variable color. Sandstone is very porous, showing various degrees of porosity.

Fauna

The fauna found at the time of the visit consisted of an unidentified spider as well as several blowflies that did not stop bothering us during the entire surveying work. Perhaps the absence of chiropterans (bats) caught my attention.

The Cova des Genet lends itself to spending a good photographic day inside, but if it is not sufficient to complete it, one only needs to visit some of the many caves found in the area.

For further reading:

TRIAS, M. & MIR, F. (1977): Les coves de la zona de Can Frasquet – Cala Varques. Endins, 4: 21-42. Palma de Mallorca.

Francesc GRACIA, Bernat CLAMOR i Joan Josep LAVERGNE. (2000): LES COVES DE CALA VARQUES (Manacor, Mallorca). Endins, 23: 41-57. Palma de Mallorca
